hypothesis: If catalytic surface compositions are represented as two-shell periodic-table vectors and encoded with a chemically informed autoencoder, then a conditional latent diffusion model conditioned on adsorbate identity, target adsorption energy, and allowed element lists will generate shell-wise compositions that better match real distributions and satisfy constraints (lower Fréchet distance, higher cosine similarity, and lower leakage) than a chemically agnostic baseline, even with reduced training data, because the latent space captures periodic chemical priors and the conditioning guides sampling toward feasible solutions.
